# Sustainable Health Ecosystems

![The complex composition features interconnected abstract forms and shards suggesting technological progression in infrastructure's environmental approach. Fine water droplets emphasize material durability under ecological stress within predicted climate change. The visual suggests the predicted influence of AI on sustainability systems.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/innovative-sustainable-infrastructure-dark-abstract-structures-showcasing.jpg "Innovative Sustainable Infrastructure Dark Abstract Structures Showcasing Environmental Resilient and Resourceful Predicted Future Concept Development")

## Fundamentals

Sustainable [Health Ecosystems Meaning → The concept of ‘Health Ecosystems’ signifies the holistic framework recognizing that human and ecological well-being are inextricably linked, forming a critical foundation for societal resilience and sustained prosperity within planetary boundaries.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/health-ecosystems/)  represent a transformative approach to healthcare, shifting from a reactive, disease-focused model to a proactive, wellness-oriented system. At its core, a [Sustainable Health Ecosystem Sustainable aquaculture → a path to food security, or a peril to our coasts? The answer lies in our choices.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/sustainable-aquaculture-and-coastal-ecosystem-health/)  prioritizes the interconnectedness of various elements influencing health outcomes. This includes not only traditional healthcare providers and facilities but also community resources, environmental factors, and individual behaviors.

The goal is to create a holistic system that promotes well-being, prevents illness, and ensures [equitable access Meaning → Ensuring fair opportunity for all to benefit from and contribute to a sustainable future, addressing historical and systemic barriers.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/equitable-access/)  to care for all members of a population. It’s about designing systems that can endure, adapt, and improve over time, without compromising the health of future generations or depleting essential resources.

A key aspect of [understanding Sustainable Health Ecosystems Informed public is key to responsible carbon removal, shaping policies & driving innovation for a sustainable future.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/public-understanding-of-carbon-removal-technologies/)  lies in recognizing the **social determinants of health**. These are the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age. Factors such as poverty, education, access to healthy food, safe housing, and social support networks significantly impact health outcomes.

A sustainable ecosystem addresses these determinants by creating partnerships and initiatives that promote [health equity Meaning → Ensuring everyone has a fair opportunity to achieve their highest level of health by removing unjust obstacles.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/health-equity/)  and reduce disparities. This may involve community-based programs, policy changes, and investments in infrastructure that supports healthy lifestyles.

The concept of [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  is also central to this model. It requires careful consideration of the environmental impact of healthcare practices. This includes reducing waste, conserving energy, and minimizing the use of harmful chemicals.

A sustainable healthcare system also invests in renewable [energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  sources, promotes green building practices, and supports sustainable supply chains. By minimizing its environmental footprint, the healthcare sector can contribute to a healthier planet for all.

![A digitally rendered futuristic object intertwines a chrome tube and skeletal organic design. This eco design explores future tech sustainability and advancements through an abstract presentation of interlocking chrome and hollow forms, reflecting the integration of technological advancements for sustainability goals in the face of tech ethics concerns. Digital sustainability takes form, envisioning resilient design, technological innovations, and ethical responsibilities within complex technological environments, and illustrates materiality’s interplay in eco conscious infrastructure development.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-futures-interwoven-exploring-innovation-resilience-and-tech-ethics.jpg "Sustainable Futures Interwoven Exploring Innovation Resilience and Tech Ethics in AI Materiality")

### Key Components of a Sustainable Health Ecosystem

To build a [Sustainable Health Meaning → Sustainable Health ensures human well-being endures by harmonizing societal progress with ecological regeneration and intergenerational equity.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/sustainable-health/)  Ecosystem, several key components must be in place. These components work together to create a comprehensive and integrated system of care:

It is also about creating a system that is resilient and adaptable to change. This requires [investing Meaning → Investing, through an ESG lens, involves allocating capital to companies that demonstrate strong environmental, social, and governance practices.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  in workforce development, promoting innovation, and fostering a culture of continuous improvement. A sustainable healthcare system is one that can withstand shocks, learn from its mistakes, and evolve to meet the changing needs of the population.

For individuals new to sustainability, understanding the interconnectedness of health, environment, and social factors is the starting point. It’s about recognizing that our health is not solely determined by medical interventions but is also shaped by the world around us. This understanding can empower individuals to make informed choices about their health and to advocate for policies that support healthy communities.

![A sleek architectural study portrays a futuristic building utilizing white and dark contrasting elements. Embedded within are spheres. Geometric shapes imply technology integration promoting AI driven enhancements.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/innovative-sustainable-architecture-futuristic-design-with-spheres-representing.jpg "Innovative Sustainable Architecture Futuristic Design with Spheres Representing Eco Friendly Technological Integration")

### Practical Steps for Building a Sustainable Health Ecosystem

Building a Sustainable Health Ecosystem requires a collaborative effort involving healthcare providers, community organizations, policymakers, and individuals. Here are some practical steps that can be taken:

By taking these steps, communities can begin to [build Sustainable Health Ecosystems PPPs → Synergizing public vision and private drive to construct the backbone of a decarbonized future via CCUS.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/public-private-partnerships-driving-ccus-infrastructure-build-out/)  that promote well-being, prevent illness, and ensure equitable access to care for all. It’s a long-term process that requires commitment, collaboration, and a shared vision for a healthier future.

One example of a sustainable practice is the implementation of telemedicine programs, which not only improve access to care for rural populations but also reduce carbon emissions associated with travel. Similarly, hospitals can reduce their environmental impact by implementing waste reduction programs, using energy-efficient equipment, and sourcing sustainable products. These actions not only benefit the environment but also improve the health and [well-being Meaning → Well-being encompasses holistic health and happiness, supported by sustainable lifestyle choices that benefit both individuals and the environment.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/)  of patients and staff.

In summary, a Sustainable Health Ecosystem is more than just a healthcare system; it is a network of interconnected elements that work together to promote health, prevent illness, and ensure equitable access to care for all. It requires a holistic approach that addresses the [social determinants Meaning → Social Determinants of Health are the foundational societal conditions and systems that profoundly shape individual and community well-being.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/social-determinants/)  of health, promotes sustainability, and fosters collaboration among stakeholders. By [building Sustainable Health Resilient health data → safeguarding global well-being in an era of uncertainty.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/building-resilient-global-health-data-infrastructure/)  Ecosystems, we can create healthier, more equitable, and more sustainable communities for generations to come.

![The sculpture highlights a call to sustainable action using contrast. A light sphere rests over a metallic base beside dark components suggesting technological advancement balanced with environmental sustainability concerns. Streamlined bands and surface detailing highlight innovative industrial material and sustainable production design with upcycled solutions.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/exploring-sustainable-material-innovation-for-circular-economy-an-examination.jpg "Exploring Sustainable Material Innovation for Circular Economy an Examination of Upcycled Design Futures")

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the foundational understanding, the intermediate perspective on Sustainable Health Ecosystems necessitates a deeper examination of the systemic interactions and feedback loops that govern their function. It is no longer sufficient to simply acknowledge the interconnectedness of various components; instead, a detailed analysis of how these components influence each other, both positively and negatively, is required. This includes understanding the complex relationships between healthcare providers, patients, community organizations, government agencies, and the environment.

At this level, the focus shifts to designing and implementing strategies that optimize the performance of the ecosystem as a whole. This requires a systems-thinking approach, which involves identifying the key drivers of health outcomes, understanding the relationships between these drivers, and developing interventions that target the root causes of problems. It also requires a willingness to experiment, learn from failures, and adapt strategies based on evidence.

A central theme in the intermediate understanding of Sustainable Health Ecosystems is the concept of **value-based care**. This approach shifts the focus from volume to value, incentivizing healthcare providers to deliver high-quality care at a lower cost. Value-based care models often involve bundled payments, capitation payments, and shared savings programs. These models encourage providers to coordinate care, prevent unnecessary hospitalizations, and improve patient outcomes.

The implementation of value-based care requires a robust data infrastructure that can track health outcomes, measure costs, and identify areas for improvement. This includes electronic health records, claims data, and patient-reported outcomes. Data analytics can be used to identify high-risk patients, predict future health needs, and personalize care plans. It also requires a culture of transparency and accountability, where providers are willing to share data and learn from each other.

![Black spheres and branching rods rest against folded black textile embodying a commitment to resource management and innovative material usage. Glossy surfaces and abstract forms speak to advances in circular design that is focused on waste reduction with futuristic technological possibilities. It is all a future vision for environmental conservation through technology and long term environmental sustainability strategies, promoting an inclusive and collaborative sustainable global change framework.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-spheres-and-fractal-branches-black-fabric-symbolizing-sustainable.jpg "Abstract Spheres and Fractal Branches Black Fabric Symbolizing Sustainable Futures Technological Advancement for Environmental Conservation")

### Integrating Technology for Enhanced Sustainability

Technology the backbone of a robust and adaptable Sustainable Health Ecosystem. Here are some key areas where technological solutions are making a significant impact:

Another critical aspect of Sustainable Health Ecosystems is the integration of **environmental sustainability** into healthcare operations. This involves reducing the environmental footprint of healthcare facilities, promoting sustainable supply chains, and investing in renewable energy sources. Hospitals can reduce their energy [consumption Meaning → Consumption refers to the use of goods and services, with a sustainable focus on mindful, responsible, and circular patterns.](https://eco.sustainability-directory.com/)  by implementing energy-efficient lighting, heating, and cooling systems. They can also reduce waste by implementing recycling programs and reducing the use of disposable products.

Sustainable supply chains involve sourcing products from companies that adhere to environmentally and socially responsible practices. This includes reducing greenhouse gas emissions, conserving water, and protecting biodiversity. Healthcare organizations can also invest in renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, to reduce their reliance on fossil fuels.

![A glossy dark surface presents a clear disc holding a central cream sphere, portraying the concept of future technology that enhances a sustainable future. The image symbolizes technological breakthroughs using advanced materials. It indicates future innovations and possible changes to promote environmental management.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/futuristic-sustainablity-concept-an-exploration-of-circular-economy-and.jpg "Futuristic Sustainablity Concept an Exploration of Circular Economy and Material Science in Technological Innovation")

### Addressing Health Equity in Sustainable Ecosystems

Health equity is a fundamental principle of Sustainable Health Ecosystems. This means ensuring that all members of the population have fair and just opportunities to be healthy. [Addressing health equity Fair trade can empower vulnerable communities to build climate resilience and foster sustainable development amidst global change.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/fair-trades-role-in-addressing-climate-change-vulnerability/)  requires identifying and addressing the social determinants of health that contribute to disparities. This includes poverty, education, housing, and access to healthy food.

One strategy for addressing health equity is to invest in **community-based programs** that target the root causes of disparities. This may involve providing job training, affordable housing, and access to healthy food. It also requires building trust and partnerships with community organizations and residents. Community health workers can play a crucial role in bridging the gap [between healthcare providers Electronics retail as service can be a sustainability game-changer, or amplify e-waste â€“ the choice is ours.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/future-of-electronics-retail-as-service-providers/)  and underserved populations.

Policy changes are also needed to address health equity. This may involve increasing funding for public health programs, expanding access to Medicaid, and implementing policies that promote healthy food choices. It also requires addressing systemic racism and discrimination, which contribute to health disparities.

An example of a successful Sustainable Health Ecosystem is the **Kaiser Permanente** model, which integrates healthcare delivery, financing, and insurance. This model allows Kaiser Permanente to control costs, improve quality, and promote prevention. It also invests in community health initiatives and addresses the social determinants of health.

Another example is the **Accountable Care Organization (ACO)** model, which brings together groups of doctors, hospitals, and other healthcare providers to coordinate care for Medicare patients. ACOs are incentivized to improve quality and reduce costs. They also share savings with Medicare.

In conclusion, the intermediate understanding of Sustainable Health Ecosystems requires a systems-thinking approach, a focus on value-based care, and a commitment to [environmental sustainability Meaning → Maintaining ecological integrity and resource availability for enduring human and planetary well-being within biophysical limits.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/environmental-sustainability/)  and health equity. It also requires a robust data infrastructure, a culture of transparency and accountability, and a willingness to experiment and learn from failures. By implementing these strategies, healthcare organizations can build Sustainable Health Ecosystems that improve health outcomes, reduce costs, and promote health equity.

The transition to sustainable ecosystems is not without its difficulties. Siloed thinking, resistance to change, and insufficient funding can impede progress. Addressing these obstacles necessitates strong leadership, effective communication, and a collaborative approach that involves all stakeholders.

A black square supports the arrangement that includes spheres, cube and a clear shard topped with an element of a dark shiny material. Symbolizing interconnectedness of data and technology for a sustainable ecosystem with efficient resource allocation, the art evokes thoughts for future material choices as a responsible step.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-sculpture-illustrating-sustainable-innovation-with-interconnected.jpg "Abstract Sculpture Illustrating Sustainable Innovation with Interconnected Transparent Geometric Forms Representing Future AI and Technological Evolution")

## Academic

At the academic level, a Sustainable Health Ecosystem is conceptualized as a [complex adaptive system exhibiting emergent properties Meaning → Novel attributes of complex systems that arise from component interactions, not predictable from individual parts.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/emergent-properties/)  that transcend the sum of its individual components. It represents a dynamic interplay of social, economic, environmental, and technological factors, intricately linked to influence population health outcomes in a perpetually evolving manner. This definition moves beyond simple descriptions of interconnectedness, demanding a rigorous examination of the underlying mechanisms, feedback loops, and power dynamics that shape the ecosystem’s trajectory and resilience.

Academically, Sustainable Health Ecosystems are viewed through the lens of various theoretical frameworks, including systems theory, complexity science, and ecological economics. Systems theory provides a foundation for understanding the interdependencies and feedback loops within the ecosystem, while complexity science highlights the unpredictable and emergent nature of its behavior. Ecological economics emphasizes the importance of integrating environmental considerations into economic decision-making, recognizing the finite nature of resources and the need for sustainable practices.

The defining academic exploration lies in the **application of mixed-methods research** to dissect the intricacies of these ecosystems. Quantitative analyses, employing sophisticated statistical modeling and econometrics, are utilized to assess the impact of specific interventions or policies on health outcomes, cost-effectiveness, and environmental sustainability. Concurrently, qualitative research methods, such as ethnography, grounded theory, and participatory action research, are employed to gain a deeper understanding of the lived experiences of individuals within the ecosystem, capturing the diverse perspectives and [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  dynamics that shape their health-seeking behaviors and access to resources.

A key area of investigation within the academic domain is the **role of policy and governance** in shaping Sustainable Health Ecosystems. This involves analyzing the impact of various policies, regulations, and incentives on health outcomes, environmental sustainability, and social equity. It also requires examining the [governance Meaning → Governance, in an ESG context, refers to the systems of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  structures and decision-making processes that influence the allocation of resources and the implementation of interventions. Academic research in this area often draws on political science, public administration, and law to provide a comprehensive understanding of the policy landscape.

### Redefining Sustainable Health Ecosystems → A Cross-Sectoral Analysis of the Built Environment

Based on a synthesis of reputable research and data, particularly from sources like Google Scholar and publications from organizations such as the World Health Organization and the United Nations Environment Programme, a refined academic definition of Sustainable Health Ecosystems can be proposed. This definition places significant emphasis on the cross-sectoral influences, particularly the **built environment**, as a critical determinant of long-term health and well-being. This perspective acknowledges that health is not solely a product of healthcare interventions but is fundamentally shaped by the physical spaces and infrastructure within which people live, work, and play.

A Sustainable Health Ecosystem, therefore, is redefined as a dynamic and interconnected network of social, economic, environmental, and technological systems, strategically designed and managed to promote population health and well-being over the long term. It prioritizes equitable access to resources and opportunities, minimizes environmental impact, and fosters resilience to shocks and stresses. A core tenet of this redefined ecosystem is the intentional integration of health considerations into all aspects of the built environment, including urban planning, transportation, housing, and infrastructure development.

The [built environment Meaning → All human-made physical surroundings and their interconnected systems, designed to support human activity and mediate interactions with the natural world.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/built-environment/)  encompasses a wide range of factors that can significantly impact health outcomes. By integrating health considerations into the design and management of the built environment, it is possible to create communities that promote health and well-being for all residents. ### Outcomes of Prioritizing the Built Environment in Sustainable Health Ecosystems

Focusing on the built environment within Sustainable Health Ecosystems has several potential positive outcomes:

The integration of health considerations into the built environment requires a shift in mindset, from viewing health as solely a medical issue to recognizing it as a fundamental aspect of community design and development. It also requires a commitment to equity, ensuring that all residents have access to the resources and opportunities they need to be healthy.

An example of a community that has successfully integrated health considerations into the built environment is **Curitiba, Brazil**. Curitiba has implemented a comprehensive urban planning strategy that prioritizes public transportation, green spaces, and pedestrian-friendly streets. As a result, Curitiba has one of the highest rates of public transportation use in the world, as well as a large network of parks and green spaces. These investments have contributed to improved air quality, reduced traffic congestion, and a higher quality of life for residents.

Another example is the **Healthy Communities Movement** in the United States, which promotes community-based initiatives to address the social determinants of health. These initiatives often involve partnerships between healthcare providers, community organizations, and government agencies to improve access to healthy food, safe housing, and quality education.

In conclusion, at the academic level, a Sustainable Health Ecosystem is best understood as a [complex adaptive system Meaning → Explores how dynamic, interconnected systems persist, adapt, and transform in response to disruptions while maintaining essential functions.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/complex-adaptive-systems-resilience/)  that requires a cross-sectoral approach, with a particular emphasis on the built environment. By integrating health considerations into all aspects of community design and development, it is possible to create communities that promote health, well-being, and equity for all residents. This requires a shift in mindset, a commitment to equity, and a collaborative approach involving all stakeholders.

The future research should prioritize longitudinal studies that track the long-term impacts of built environment interventions on health outcomes, as well as studies that examine the cost-effectiveness of different approaches. Additionally, research is needed to develop and validate metrics for assessing the sustainability of health ecosystems, taking into account environmental, social, and economic factors.

It is important to acknowledge the limitations of this redefined definition. The complexity of Sustainable Health Ecosystems makes it difficult to isolate the impact of any single factor, such as the built environment. Additionally, the lack of standardized metrics for measuring sustainability and health equity makes it challenging to compare different ecosystems and track progress over time. However, by acknowledging these limitations and continuing to refine our understanding of these complex systems, we can move closer to creating communities that promote health and well-being for all.

In the future, Sustainable Health Ecosystems will likely be characterized by even greater integration of technology, personalized medicine, and community-based interventions. The of big data and artificial intelligence will enable more precise identification of health risks and tailored interventions, while the use of wearable sensors and remote monitoring devices will allow for continuous tracking of patient health and proactive management of chronic conditions. The focus will shift from reactive treatment to proactive prevention, with a greater emphasis on promoting healthy lifestyles and addressing the social determinants of health.

# Primary healthcare’s carbon footprint and sustainable strategies to mitigate its contribution: a scoping review

[*BMC Health Services Research*](/)
**volume 24**, Article number: 1630 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

3207 Accesses

5 Citations

2 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

The escalating climate crisis poses a significant threat to global public health. The healthcare sector, designed to protect human health is a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions, and thus, a key driver of climate degradation. This paradox endangers both planetary and human health, making the decarbonization of healthcare, including primary care, critical. However, research on primary care’s contribution to emissions and strategies for mitigation remains limited.

### Aim

This scoping review aimed to map how primary care contributes to healthcare’s environmental footprint and determine contributing factors. Additionally, it sought to identify existing and innovative strategies to reduce the carbon footprint of primary healthcare.

### Methods

A comprehensive strategy was developed to systematically search both published databases and grey literature. Key terms were identified and employed in the exploration of relevant databases and internet search engines.

### Results

An initial search yielded 246 published articles and 25 grey literature sources. 14 additional articles were included following forward and backward searching of prominent authors and key articles. After screening and full-text review, 39 articles and 12 reports/toolkits were included. The majority of sources were opinion pieces, with limited quantitative, observational, or qualitative studies.

Primary care’s carbon footprint can be classified into clinical and non-clinical sources, with significant impacts from pharmaceuticals and inhaler propellant gases. Contributing factors include limited knowledge of emission sources, lack of awareness of sustainable practices, low prioritization of sustainability, barriers including ethical concerns and over-medicalization.

Identified strategies to reduce emissions include decarbonization of patient care, increasing education and awareness, implementing non-clinical decarbonization efforts, and conducting more research to support sustainable initiatives. Developing metrics to track progress and securing policy supports to improve adoption and implementation were also highlighted as critical.

### Conclusion

The identification of sources of carbon hotspots in primary care is an essential precursor to enable the development of targeted decarbonization strategies. Decarbonizing primary care requires a multifaceted approach that addresses the underlying factors driving unsustainable practices. This would allow healthcare professionals to effectively balance the provision of high-quality patient care, while reducing their environmental impact, ultimately improving both human and planetary health.

### Inclusion criteria

The review encompassed studies and reports that primarily addressed two areas: (1) assessments of the carbon impact or analyses of the carbon footprint associated with primary healthcare services, and (2) the exploration of sustainable strategies for mitigating the carbon footprint within the context of primary care. Studies solely focused on tertiary care or hospital settings were excluded. Outpatient services delivered within the community setting were considered for inclusion if they represented a deliberate transfer of services from hospital to community settings. Studies published prior to 2010 were excluded. Eligible studies were required to be available in English and accessible in full text.

### Types of information sources

All published existing evidence including primary research studies, scoping reviews, systematic reviews, quantitative and qualitative studies together with relevant editorials or other such articles from the peer-reviewed literature were sought. Additionally, grey literature, including government or organizational reports that met the established inclusion criteria were considered. Opinion pieces, perspectives and letters were considered when they offered compelling perspectives or novel insights that addressed the research question and explored potential avenues for change. The latter sources were analyzed through the lens of identifying potential for change and novel perspectives.

### Search strategy

A comprehensive search strategy was developed in collaboration with an experienced information specialist to identify published studies and reports and further refined through investigator discussion. MEDLINE, Scopus, Embase, CINAHL, Global Health, and Health Business Elite databases were included. The search strategy, including all identified keywords and index terms, was adapted for each individual database. The final search strategy for MEDLINE can be found in Supplementary Material [2](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8#MOESM2). Forward and backward citation tracing of key articles and prominent authors was conducted to identify any additional relevant articles not previously identified in the search strategy.

The grey literature search plan was developed in accordance with Godin et al.’s [[29](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8#ref-CR29 "Godin K, Stapleton J, Kirkpatrick SI, Hanning RM, Leatherdale ST. Applying systematic review search methods to the grey literature: a case study examining guidelines for school-based breakfast programs in Canada. Syst Rev. 2015;4:138.")] application of systematic search methods to grey literature [[29](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8#ref-CR29 "Godin K, Stapleton J, Kirkpatrick SI, Hanning RM, Leatherdale ST. Applying systematic review search methods to the grey literature: a case study examining guidelines for school-based breakfast programs in Canada. Syst Rev. 2015;4:138.")]. This plan incorporated four discrete searching strategies: (1) grey literature databases, (2) customized Google search engines, (3) targeted websites, and (4) consulting with relevant experts and prominent active organizations within the field of research. These complementary strategies were employed to minimize the risk of omitting relevant sources and to establish current international and national governmental and organizational policy context, motivation, and appetite for change among those working in the area with the aim of establishing the carbon footprint in primary care and resultant mitigation strategies.

### Selection of sources of evidence

All citations retrieved were collated and uploaded into EndNote® (version 21.2) bibliography software and duplicates were removed. Titles and abstracts were screened by the lead investigator against the inclusion criteria. Due to the frequent absence of abstracts in grey literature documents, screening was conducted using available materials such as item executive summaries, or tables of contents. Full text articles were retrieved for all remaining articles and reports and further assessed independently for relevance by two members of the research team. Any dissension regarding study eligibility was resolved through discussion, and consensus reached. Reasons for excluding studies at the full-text stage that did not meet the inclusion criteria were recorded and reported in the final scoping review. The PRISMA-ScR flow diagram was used to present the search results and the study selection process [[30](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8#ref-CR30 "Page MJ, McKenzie JE, Bossuyt PM, Boutron I, Hoffmann TC, Mulrow CD, et al. The PRISMA 2020 statement: an updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. BMJ. 2021;372:n71.")].

### Data extraction

A standardized data extraction chart was developed to capture relevant information including author, year, country, clinical setting, study design, research aim and key findings. Three investigators independently charted the extracted data, identifying emergent themes relevant to the research topic of carbon assessment and mitigation strategies within primary care. Validation of extracted data involved comparing results and resolving differences through consensus.

### Data items

Data were extracted that contained formal results related to the source, location, and contributing factors of the carbon footprint of primary care. Additionally, data regarding mitigation strategies encompassing current and potential strategies, along with commentary addressing barriers, facilitators, and implementation strategies was extracted.

### Synthesis of results

Following individual data extraction, the investigators convened and collaboratively refined the themes identified through an iterative process and grouped them in accordance with answering the three key topics of the research question: (1) how and where primary care contributes to its carbon footprint, (2) why these activities within primary healthcare contribute to its carbon footprint, and (3) identifying mitigation strategies that can be implemented to reduce to primary care’s carbon footprint. The data was collated using tables and descriptive text and analyzed to comprehensively assess both the nature of primary care’s environmental impact and potential solutions for mitigating it.

### Critical appraisal of sources of evidence

Peter’s et al*.* states that critical appraisal is typically not required to be undertaken in a scoping review because the objective of a scoping review is to map the existing evidence rather than deliver a synthesized and clinically significant response to a specific question [[24](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8#ref-CR24 "Peters MDJ, Marnie C, Tricco AC, Pollock D, Munn Z, Alexander L, et al. Updated methodological guidance for the conduct of scoping reviews. JBI Evid. 2020;18:2119–26.")]. Therefore, formal critical appraisal was not undertaken.

## Results

### Selection of sources of evidence

An initial search of relevant electronic databases yielded 246 articles. Following the removal of duplicate entries (*n* = 40), the remaining 206 articles underwent title and abstract screening. A complementary search of grey literature sources identified 12 reports, 10 toolkits, two guidelines and one framework. During the screening process, a total of 159 articles did not meet the predefined inclusion criteria, primarily due to their sole applicability to hospital care settings. Two articles were excluded because the full text was unavailable, and two toolkits were excluded due to restricted online accessibility. Fourteen additional articles were identified for full-text review based on backward and forward citation analysis of key studies and prominent authors. Following a full-text review of the remaining 59 articles by three independent reviewers, 39 were considered to meet the inclusion criteria, and of the reports, toolkits, guidelines and the one framework, 12 were deemed eligible for data extraction (Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8#Fig1)). Details of the reasons for exclusion of studies following the full-text review are provided in Supplementary Material [3](/articles/10.1186/s12913-024-12068-8#MOESM3). The most frequent reasons for exclusion pertained to the limited applicability of studies to the primary care setting, with studies focusing primarily on outpatient clinics or specialist secondary care interventions.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12913-024-12068-8/MediaObjects/12913_2024_12068_Fig1_HTML.png)

# Building a sustainable health-care system

![](/sites/default/files/2023-04/building_a_sustainable_health_care_system_mobile.png)
![](/sites/default/files/styles/gallery_1050x600/public/2023-04/building_a_sustainable_health_care_system_desktop.png?itok=S8bLC-ZZ)

In the past few years, British Columbians have faced increasingly challenging environmental and climate related events. Extreme heat, wildfires and floods are a few examples of disasters that have significantly affected those living in the Interior and the rest of B.C.

This interdependence between our health and the environment compels us to continue our work to reduce our environmental footprint toward a low-carbon future.

As we mark Earth Day 2023, here are a few highlights of our environmental sustainability efforts in the Interior region. You can also [view and download a visual infographic](https://www.interiorhealth.ca/sites/default/files/PDFS/building-a-sustainable-health-care-system.pdf) of our projects and initiatives.

### In this Story…

#### We are committed to eliminating Styrofoam

![A blue illustration of a styrofoam container overlaid by a circle and slash](/sites/default/files/inline-images/styrofoam_image.png)

![A blue illustration of a styrofoam container overlaid by a circle and slash](/sites/default/files/inline-images/styrofoam_image.png)

On Earth Day 2022, IH committed to eliminating Styrofoam use in food services. We are working with our vendors and the Provincial Health Services Authority to identify alternatives that maintain the quality of patient care and safety.

#### We are recovering waste anesthetic gases

![An image of a blue cloud and a carbon dioxide CO2 element with an arrow pointing down](/sites/default/files/inline-images/co2-image.png)

![An image of a blue cloud and a carbon dioxide CO2 element with an arrow pointing down](/sites/default/files/inline-images/co2-image.png)

Anesthetic gases are a potent source of greenhouse gas emissions. They reduce air quality and contribute to climate change. They can also present an occupational health and safety hazard to operating room staff if not properly ventilated. In Kamloops, Royal Inland Hospital’s 11 operating rooms now [recover almost all anesthetic gases](https://www.interiorhealth.ca/media/interior-health-invests-greener-future) using canisters, which are then sent away for processing. Read the [media release](https://www.interiorhealth.ca/media/interior-health-reduces-its-carbon-footprint-collecting-waste-anesthetic-gases) here.

#### We’re relying on nature to make our facilities more environmentally and patient friendly

![An illustration of three blue flowers that look like daisies](/sites/default/files/inline-images/garden_image.png)

![An illustration of three blue flowers that look like daisies](/sites/default/files/inline-images/garden_image.png)

Invermere’s Sustainability Committee planted a wildflower garden to reduce water use, increase pollination, and provide a healing space for patients.

#### We’re practising environmentally sustainable health care

![A simple illustration of an open blue book with stylized lines and an image](/sites/default/files/inline-images/guidebook_image_1.png)

![A simple illustration of an open blue book with stylized lines and an image](/sites/default/files/inline-images/guidebook_image_1.png)

Family physician [Dr. Ilona Hale](https://www.interiorhealth.ca/stories/we-are-ih-climate-change-and-nature-are-physicians-passion), a clinical researcher and avid gardener in Kimberley, has developed a guide for more environmentally sustainable practices in primary care, focusing on four principles of sustainable practice: prevention, patient self-care, lean service delivery, and low carbon alternatives.

#### We’re saving on our energy use and costs

![An illustration of a blue electric cord with a plug on one end and a leaf on the other in the shape of a U](/sites/default/files/inline-images/energy_image.png)

![An illustration of a blue electric cord with a plug on one end and a leaf on the other in the shape of a U](/sites/default/files/inline-images/energy_image.png)

In Cranbrook, East Kootenay Regional Hospital has been working with an external building systems energy specialist to complete a number of upgrades to the building systems. Once these energy conservation measures are implemented, we estimate we will achieve a 23 per cent reduction on greenhouse gas emissions, and realize energy savings of 19 per cent.

#### We’re planning and implementing changes

![An illustration of a blue lightbulb next to two electricity bolts and with two arrows circling around](/sites/default/files/inline-images/lightbulb_image.png)

![An illustration of a blue lightbulb next to two electricity bolts and with two arrows circling around](/sites/default/files/inline-images/lightbulb_image.png)

We’ve released the 2022–23 Strategic Energy Management Plan that outlines how we will meet our long-term energy and greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets. In 2023, we have also developed the Climate Change and Sustainability Roadmap.

#### We’re getting our staff involved

![An illustration of three blue human head and shoulder figures](/sites/default/files/inline-images/committee_image.png)

![An illustration of three blue human head and shoulder figures](/sites/default/files/inline-images/committee_image.png)

Sustainability Committees have been established in Salmon Arm, Vernon, Kelowna, Penticton and Invermere. The number of staff engaged with local committees has increased 166 per cent in the past year.

Each day, Interior Health contributes to enhancing a culture of sustainability through transparency, communication and understanding that individuals can and do make a difference. In 2023 and beyond, we will continue to strive for meaningful environmental improvements in our communities and across our operations.
